title: Summer temperature anomalies
subtitle: The difference between a month's average surface temperature from the mean temperature of the same month during the period 1991-2020, measured in degrees Celsius.
note: Note that the seasons are represented according to Northern Hemisphere months. In the Southern Hemisphere, these months correspond to opposite seasons.
caption:
Summer temperature anomalies

The difference between a month's average surface temperature from the mean temperature of the same month during the period 1991-2020, measured in degrees Celsius.

This visualization presents three bar charts tracking global summer temperature anomalies for June, July, and August from 1940 through 2025. The vertical axes measure the difference in degrees Celsius from the 1991-2020 mean, with a zero line separating negative anomalies (blue bars) from positive anomalies (orange bars). In all three months, the period from 1940 to the early 1980s is dominated by blue bars extending downward, frequently reaching between -0.2°C and -0.6°C. A transition occurs around the 1980s and 1990s where bars begin to cluster near the zero line, showing a mix of slightly positive and negative values. From roughly 2014 onward, the charts display a consistent sequence of orange bars rising above the zero line. In June, recent bars reach heights between 0.2°C and 0.6°C. July shows a similar pattern, with the most recent bars extending into the 0.4°C to 0.6°C range. August follows this trend, with the final bars on the right side of the chart reaching approximately 0.5°C. The visual progression across all three panels moves from predominantly negative values in the mid-20th century to consistently positive values in the most recent years shown. The data represents Northern Hemisphere summer months, which correspond to opposite seasons in the Southern Hemisphere.
